name: Squirtle
route: Freshwater banks, reed beds, flooded cellars, old canals, abandoned river towns, storm-flooded ruins, and quiet coastal inlets
habitat: Shallow freshwater or brackish shelter with mud banks, submerged debris, air pockets, and safe nesting ledges above floodwater
classification: Tiny Turtle Pokemon / Juvenile Shoreline Chelonian
behaviorTemperament: Curious, social, and deceptively gentle when young. Wild colonies are playful at the surface but territorial beneath it.
diet: Aquatic plants, algae, small fish, insects, mollusks, carrion scraps, and food stolen from camps near the waterline
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular near safe banks. More active at dusk, during rain, and after floods.
bestTimeToFind: Dusk near reed beds, old docks, flooded ruins, or shallow pools where shells tap against stone
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Individuals may approach humans, but colony boundaries are difficult to recognize from shore.
populationStatus: Uncommon in settled waterways but locally stable where ruins, marshes, and protected nesting sites remain
description: A small aquatic reptilian Pokemon with a hardening shell, webbed limbs, lungs, and powerful mouth-spray ability. In real-world field terms, it is safest when treated as a social shoreline animal rather than a toy-like starter.
lore:
Squirtle is the reason old river villages kept bells beside their wells.

Not for fire. Not for raiders. Not for storms.

For splashing.

A single splash in the night meant a child had wandered too close to the bank. Two splashes meant something had come out of the water. Three meant the village was already counting its missing.

Modern starter programs describe Squirtle as loyal, playful, social, and easier to handle than most young Water-types. That is true. It is also the safest lie ever printed in a beginnerâ€™s field guide. Squirtle is friendly because it is young. It is curious because it has not yet learned fear. It follows humans because humans are warm, noisy, and careless around shorelines.

A wild Squirtle will often approach camps at dusk, eyes wide, shell wet, little hands raised as if asking for food. It may chirp, splash, or roll onto its back to appear harmless. Many are harmless. Some have been hand-fed by fishermen for generations. Some will sleep under docks, chase thrown pebbles, and nudge lost objects back toward shore.

But Squirtle live in colonies, and colonies have rules older than kindness.

The first rule is that anything near the water belongs partly to the water.

Food dropped near a bank is taken. Nets left overnight are cut or dragged under. Boots, knives, toys, bones, and wedding rings all vanish into the reeds. People once believed Squirtle collected shiny objects out of mischief. Rangers now believe these objects are boundary markers. Squirtle colonies arrange stolen items beneath the surface in half-circles around nesting zones, creating underwater warnings that only they recognize.

A person who crosses those markers may never know they have entered protected ground.

Squirtle rarely kill by biting. Their teeth are not the primary danger. Their danger is patience, water, and numbers. A lone Squirtle will retreat from an adult human. A group will not. They circle silently beneath the surface, using their shells as shields and their small bodies as moving stones. Then one surfaces close enough to be seen.

The mistake is looking at that one.

The others take the ankles.

Survivors describe the sensation as weeds tightening around their legs. Then the bank gives way. Then the water closes over the ears and everything becomes muffled except the tapping of shells against rocks.

Squirtle do not understand drowning as murder. To them, water is safety. If a creature thrashes, it must be frightened. If it breathes badly, it must be held still. If it screams, it must be taken deeper where the colony can surround and calm it. This instinct makes them excellent rescuers when trained and terrible hazards when wild.

Many old drownings blamed on weeds, mud holes, or river spirits are now suspected Squirtle defensive incidents.

The darkest reports involve children.

Not because Squirtle hunt them, but because Squirtle seem to mistake them for vulnerable colony members. Small children playing near freshwater may be approached, splashed, and gently herded toward shallow pools. If the child laughs and follows, the Squirtle may attempt to â€œteachâ€ them to submerge. In several rural Kanto cases, children were found alive in reed beds after hours missing, soaked, hypothermic, and surrounded by Squirtle that refused to let adults approach.

In worse cases, only the toys came back.

Villagers once carved small wooden shells and hung them over doors after a child drowned. The charm was not meant to ward Squirtle away. It was an apology. It meant: this house has already paid the river.

Squirtle itself carries a strange innocence through all of this. A captured wild specimen may become deeply attached to a handler, especially one who feeds it, polishes its shell, and gives it safe water access. It learns names quickly. It recognizes routines. It will sleep near boots, packs, or bedding, guarding possessions the same way it guards river stones. Many trainers consider Squirtle one of the most emotionally reliable starter PokÃ©mon.

That bond is real.

But so is the pull of the water.

Even domesticated Squirtle may become agitated during heavy rain. They scratch doors, stare at drains, and press their faces against windows when runoff begins flowing in the street. In flood season, a Squirtle may attempt to drag bedding, food, and even sleeping humans toward what it considers safer ground. Usually that means higher ground if trained well.

If trained poorly, it means water.

The shell is another source of folklore. A young Squirtleâ€™s shell is soft at hatching and hardens over time. Old coastal families believed a Squirtle carried its first home on its back because the sea had stolen the place where it was born. There is a reason that belief lasted. Squirtle often appear after floods, tsunamis, dam failures, and shipwrecks. They gather where human structures break and water enters places it was never meant to reach.

To a Squirtle, a flooded cellar is a cave.

A sunken chapel is shelter.

A drowned town is a reef.

Divers exploring abandoned settlements in old river basins have reported Squirtle nests inside upstairs bedrooms, schoolhouses, and collapsed kitchens. The creatures decorate these nests with bright fragments of human life: buttons, marbles, spoons, coins, belt buckles, glass beads, religious medals, and teeth polished smooth by current.

One ranger found a nursery colony living inside the second floor of a submerged farmhouse. The nest was lined with childrenâ€™s shoes.

No bodies were recovered.

The official warning for Squirtle habitats is simple:

Do not camp below the flood mark.

Do not follow splashing after dark.

Do not let children feed wild specimens.

If a Squirtle gives you something from the water, accept it and leave.

If it takes something from you, do not reach in after it.

If the water goes quiet, step back before you look down.

loreSummary: A river-child species associated with wells, drowned villages, and colony boundary objects beneath the surface.
survivalUse: Can provide clean water pressure for rinsing tools, assist with shallow-water rescue, retrieve floating supplies, deter small predators, and help locate air pockets in flooded structures.
handlingWarning: Never let children feed wild Squirtle. Never stand between a Squirtle and deep water, and never reach into water after an object it has taken.
encounterAdvice: Stay above the flood mark, accept returned objects without bending over the water, and back away if splashing suddenly stops.
firstAid: Treat near-drowning, hypothermia, ankle trauma, and aspiration first. Remove the person from the waterline before assessing bites or shell impact bruising.
companionSuitability: Good for careful handlers with water access and strict shoreline safety rules
fieldBehaviors: Herds children or weak animals toward shallow pools
Presses shell against trusted companions for reassurance
signsOfPresence: Stolen objects arranged below clear water
Tiny blue shapes in upper windows of flooded structures
Bubbles rising from reed beds at dusk
equipment: First-aid kit for hypothermia and drowning response
fieldNote: A lone Squirtle on the bank may be harmless. A quiet bank with one visible Squirtle and no bird noise should be treated as an active colony edge.
originStory:
Squirtle was formally recorded in 1812, five years after Bulbasaur entered the scientific record and seven years before Ivysaur was confirmed.

Unlike Bulbasaur, Squirtle was not discovered deep in forest territory. It was documented in a place humans had already ruined.

The first accepted report came from the drowned lower district of Old Harrow Quay, a Kanto river settlement abandoned after a series of spring floods destroyed its levees. For years afterward, fishermen claimed small blue â€œshell childrenâ€ lived in the upper windows of submerged houses. They were said to knock from inside the flooded rooms at night, tapping on glass from below the waterline.

Naturalists dismissed the stories as grief folklore. The flood had taken dozens of people, including children, and the idea of little faces in the water was considered a local mourning myth.

Then the bells started ringing.

Old Harrow Quay still had a chapel tower standing above the floodwater. Its bell rope hung down into the drowned nave, unreachable from the outside. On windless nights, the bell rang anyway. Not loudly. Just once or twice, soft enough to make listeners doubt themselves. Villagers believed the dead were asking to be counted.

In 1812, a survey crew entered the chapel by boat.

They found the rope pulled through a broken side window and tied around a nest.

Inside the flooded chapel, beneath the floating remains of hymn boards and rotted pews, lived a colony of small shelled PokÃ©mon. Their eggs were hidden in the bell loft where floodwater could not reach them. Young Squirtle slept in pockets of air beneath the rafters. Adults, or what researchers then assumed were adults, guarded the lower levels and pushed floating debris into barricades.

The bell had not been rung by ghosts.

It had been rung by Squirtle tugging the rope whenever predators entered the chapel.

The first captured specimen was small, blue, and remarkably calm. It did not bite. It did not flee. It sat in a basin of water aboard the survey boat and watched the researchers with a patient, almost solemn expression. When one sailor coughed and leaned over the rail, the Squirtle sprayed water into his face, then chirped until he stepped back from the edge.

The crew interpreted this as play.

The next day, the same Squirtle dragged a sleeping deckhand by the sleeve toward a rain barrel during a storm.

That was the first recorded hint of the speciesâ€™ rescue-drowning confusion.

Early anatomy work revealed that Squirtle occupied a rare middle ground between land and water. It possessed lungs, strong swimming muscles, webbed hands and feet, and a shell that hardened with age. Its body was built for shorelines, not open ocean. It could walk, climb muddy banks, hide in reeds, and launch pressurized water from its mouth with enough force to knock loose bark from trees.

The shell was the key.

Researchers initially believed the shell was simple armor. Later studies showed it functioned as protection, buoyancy control, water storage support, and social identity. Squirtle recognize one another by shell marks, scratches, mineral stains, and growth patterns. In wild colonies, shell-touching is a greeting, a warning, and a form of reassurance. A frightened Squirtle may press its shell against another member of the group to calm itself.

This is why captured Squirtle often become attached to packs, crates, helmets, shields, and human furniture. They instinctively treat hard curved objects as social substitutes.

The Old Harrow specimens were not named Squirtle at first. The earliest field notes called them â€œQuay-shells,â€ â€œriver turtles,â€ or â€œwater imps.â€ The modern name came later from dockworkers who described the young ones as â€œsquirting turtlesâ€ after they learned to spray anyone who tried to steal fish from the drying racks.

By 1815, Squirtle colonies had been identified in several river mouths, marshlands, and coastal ruins. Many appeared in places with recent flooding. This led to the first major theory of Squirtle origin: that their ancestors were small freshwater reptiles displaced by repeated river disasters. Those ancestors survived by becoming amphibious scavengers, nesting in hollow logs, beaver dams, cave mouths, and eventually human structures.

But the darker theory came from Old Harrow Quay.

A naturalist named Mara Vell proposed that Squirtle did not merely adapt to flooded human spaces. They learned from them. The species became successful because human settlements created perfect artificial reefs: walls to block current, upper floors for eggs, cellars for cool water, chimneys for air pockets, and debris fields full of hiding places.

In other words, Squirtle may have spread because people kept building towns where water wanted to return.

This theory remains controversial, but modern records support part of it. Squirtle populations often rise after controlled reservoirs, abandoned canals, broken aqueducts, and flooded mines become available. They are not drawn to tragedy in a supernatural sense. They are drawn to shelter, water, and objects that can be arranged into colony boundaries.

Humans call these places ruins.

Squirtle call them nurseries.

The 1812 Old Harrow colony also explains why later evolutions took longer to document. Most Squirtle observed by humans were young shoreline members. Larger evolved forms withdrew into deeper water, older canals, sea caves, and protected nesting routes. Wartortle would not be formally separated from Squirtle until later, when researchers realized the long-tailed blue guardians seen around older colonies were not a regional variant, but the next stage of the line.

Blastoise would take longer still.

People had stories of cannon-backed monsters before they had proof. Sailors swore that some reefs fired water hard enough to break masts. Harbor guards reported iron plates bent inward by invisible pressure. Shipwreck survivors described a low shape beneath the waves with two black openings on its back.

For years, science ignored them.

Science often does that when the ocean keeps the evidence.

The accepted Squirtle-line chronology now begins here:

1812: Squirtle formally recorded at Old Harrow Quay after the chapel-bell colony survey.

The later records will build from that flood site, because the Squirtle line is not just a water evolution family.

It is a history of what happens when living things inherit the places humans lose to water.

Squirtle is the child at the edge of the river.

Wartortle is the thing watching from the reeds.

Blastoise is the reason old sailors stopped laughing at drowned bells.
originSummary: First formally recorded in 1812 at Old Harrow Quay after a chapel-bell colony survey revealed nesting Squirtle inside flooded human ruins.

evolutionLine.name: Squirtle
evolutionLine.note: Tiny Turtle Water-type. Juvenile shoreline colony member with shell withdrawal and pressurized mouth-spray behavior.

evolutionLine.name: Wartortle
evolutionLine.note: Turtle Water-type. Territorial guardian stage with stronger limbs, longer tail, ear plumes, and heavier water-pressure biology.

evolutionLine.name: Blastoise
evolutionLine.note: Shellfish Water-type. Final armored pressure-cannon stage with Mega and Gigantamax battle phenomena.

mainLine.name: Squirtle
mainLine.note: Juvenile shoreline survivor. Shell hardens after hatching and water projection begins early.

mainLine.name: Wartortle
mainLine.note: Guardian stage. Ear and tail plumes help balance and current-sensing during fast swimming.

mainLine.name: Blastoise
mainLine.note: Final heavy-shell stage. Carries high-pressure shell cannons and functions as a living harbor weapon.

target.name: Mega Blastoise
target.note: Temporary form with intensified cannon output, Mega Launcher ability, and increased shell mass.

target.name: Gigantamax Blastoise
target.note: Rare battle phenomenon. Treat water output and recoil forces as disaster-scale hazards.

prey: Small fish, aquatic insects, mollusks, tadpoles, exposed eggs, carrion scraps, and camp food
territoryRange: Usually 0.25 to 2 square miles of connected bank, reed, dock, cellar, or flooded ruin habitat
lifeCycle: Hatches with a soft shell that hardens quickly, grows through social colony behavior, and evolves into Wartortle at level 16.
containmentRequirements: Secure pond or large aquatic enclosure, dry basking platform, escape-safe drain covers, no child access without supervision, and no direct connection to public stormwater.
ecologicalRole: Controls aquatic insects and small fish, recycles flood carrion, moves seeds between wetlands, and converts ruined human structures into nesting habitat.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Shell rot, waterborne bacteria, algae overgrowth, respiratory stress in dirty water, and zoonotic contamination are concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are hidden above floodwater in air pockets, bell lofts, upper rooms, hollow logs, or raised reed mats. Colonies defend nest routes more strongly than individuals.
predators: Fearow, Arbok, large fish Pokemon, Blastoise-line territorial rivals, humans, and flood-displaced carnivores
emergencyResponse: Move victims away from the bank, count all people twice, avoid entering the water, use throw ropes instead of wading, and call trained Water-type handlers for colony retrieval.
legalStatus: Water-type handling permit recommended or required near public waterways
lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years before evolution or predation. Managed care: often longer with clean water and shell care.
socialStructure: Colony juveniles under Wartortle guardians. Young specimens bond strongly to caretakers but remain influenced by water and group boundaries.

fieldCapabilities.waterPressureLevel: Juvenile but hazardous at close range
fieldCapabilities.effectiveSprayRange: About 10 to 20 ft for defensive spray
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Fast short swim and quick shoreline dash
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: High in water, moderate on land
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Low, but can launch from banks or rocks
fieldCapabilities.balance: Excellent in water due to shell shape and tail control
fieldCapabilities.breathHolding: Extended submersion capability compared with ordinary reptiles

trainingRisk: Near-drowning incidents, boundary aggression, theft behavior, drain fixation, rescue-drowning confusion, shell disease, and water contamination
physiology: Squirtle converts stored breath and muscular shell compression into pressurized water bursts. Its shell functions as armor, buoyancy control, and social identity marker, while webbed limbs and a curled tail aid swimming.

keyFacts.tailLength: Short curled tail used for balance and water display
keyFacts.habitat: Freshwater banks, flooded ruins, reed beds, and old docks
keyFacts.lifespan: Wild: 1 to 12 years before evolution or predation. Managed: variable with water quality.

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Strong bonding potential but high water-safety management required.
physicalCharacteristics: Small blue bipedal reptilian body, light belly, red-brown shell, webbed limbs, curled tail, broad eyes, and a shell that hardens after hatching.
dietRestrictions: Avoid polluted water, excess salted human food, dry-only housing, dirty tanks, and low-calcium diets that weaken shell development.

realismNotes.interpretation: Game powers are treated as biological pressure, shell, swimming, and weather-response systems that could plausibly exist in a field-guide version of the real world.
realismNotes.keptGameRequirements: Water type, Torrent, Rain Dish as hidden ability, Squirtle to Wartortle at level 16, Wartortle to Blastoise at level 36, and Blastoise special forms.
realismNotes.safetyFraming: Water projection and shell impact are treated as physical hazards, with drowning and structural damage handled as the primary real-world risks.

researchSources.name: Pokemon.com PokÃ©dex
researchSources.usedFor: Official species category, height, weight, type, and listed standard ability.

researchSources.name: PokÃ©mon Database
researchSources.usedFor: National numbers, hidden ability, base stats, type defenses, move references, and evolution levels.

researchSources.name: User provided lore
researchSources.usedFor: Dark lore, origin stories, historical discovery chronology, and real-life field-guide tone.

researchSources.usedFor: Required structure, field names, and Field PokÃ©dex style.
